comments.body: whats wrong with your current solution?

answers.body:
If I understand the question correctly then this seems to be the right way to me, all you then need to do is either:

List<String> strings = new ArrayList<String>();
strings.add("10");
strings.add("20");
strings.add("30");

A.put(strings);

Or you can:

A.put(Arrays.asList("10", "20", "30"));

Like this -

LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>> A = new LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>>();
List<String> list = new ArrayList<String>();
list.add("10");
list.add("20");
list.add("30");
A.put("a", list);

Like :

List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add("abc");
list.add("xyz");
// ....

Map<String,List<String>> map = new HashMap<>();
map.put("Key", list);
as I know that, you can use Apache MultiValueMap. It meets your requirement.http://commons.apache.org/collections/apidocs/org/apache/commons/collections/map/MultiValueMap.html

Here is a program.

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.LinkedHashMap;
import java.util.List;


public class Test {

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>> A = new LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>>();
        List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
        list.add("1");
        A.put("1", list);

        //add new values
        list = A.get("1");
        if(list!=null){
            list.add("2");
        }else{
            list = new ArrayList<String>();
            list.add("2");
        }
        A.put("1", list);

    }

}

You can replace List with TreeSet and if the all values are integer then it will be better to use Integer instead of String

Here in example taken Integer type while just replace it with String it will work fine as well.

public static void main(String[] args) {
        LinkedHashMap<String, TreeSet<Integer>> lhm = new LinkedHashMap<>();
        TreeSet<Integer> set = new TreeSet<>();
        set.add(20);
        set.add(10);
        set.add(30);
        set.add(50);
        set.add(70);
        set.add(60);
        set.add(90);
        set.addAll(Arrays.asList(22,33,44,55));
        lhm.put("A",set);
        
        System.out.println(lhm);
    }

You are on a good start. Beacuse your value are stored in an ArrayList, the map.put() operation will append the new value to the arraylist.

import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.LinkedHashMap;


public class Example{
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        LinkedHashMap<String, ArrayList<String>> map = new LinkedHashMap<>();

        //Create a list for A
        ArrayList<String> someValuesForA = new ArrayList<>();
        someValuesForA.add("10");
        someValuesForA.add("20");
        someValuesForA.add("30");

        //Add A and the list to the map
        map.put("A", someValuesForA);
        System.out.println(map.get("A"));

        //Add a new value to the list
        map.get("A").add("40");
        System.out.println(map.get("A"));

        //In case you want to add a key with no initial values
        map.put("B", new ArrayList<>());
    }
}

tl;dr

In modern Java, use Map#computeIfAbsent. And other niceties.

Map < String, SequencedCollection < String > > map = new HashMap <>( );
Function < String, SequencedCollection < String > > ifAbsentFunction = _ -> new TreeSet <>( );

map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Apple" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Apricot" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "B" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Blueberry" );
…

See post Map vs. Multimap by Donald Raab, creator of Eclipse Collections.

Multimap

A Map is a collection of key-value pairs. If you want multiple values per key, then you need a Multimap.

The Java Collections Framework does not provide directly for the concept of Multimap. But you can define the value part of the pair as a collection. You have done just that in your Question, LinkedHashMap< String , List < String > >.

In earlier Java, creating and filling that inner collection was clumsy, requiring multiple lines to see if the value is null, if so instantiate, then access the inner collection.

Map#computeIfAbsent

Handling the inner collection in a map with inner collection became easier in Java 8+.

The Map interface gained the method computeIfAbsent. We can use that method to more compactly test for the existence of a collection as the value. If absent we can "compute" to instantiate an empty collection object.

Let's try this technique by collecting the names of fruit by their first letter.

Map < String, List < String > > map = new HashMap <>( );

map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , key -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Apple" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , key -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Apricot" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "B" , key -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Blueberry" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "C" , key -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Cantaloupe" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "B" , key -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Banana" );

That works.

map.toString(): {A=[Apple, Apricot], B=[Blueberry, Banana], C=[Cantaloupe]}

Unnamed variable _

The variable name key is never used in our example code. So we can replace that with an underscore character as an unnamed variable in Java 22+. See JEP 456: Unnamed Variables & Patterns.

map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , _ -> new ArrayList <>( ) ).add( "Apple" );
…

Function object

Notice how we repeat that lambda code instantiating the new ArrayList. We can replace that with a single object, a Function object.

Function < String, List < String > > ifAbsentFunction = _ -> new ArrayList <>( );

Then replace the lambda with that variable.

map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Apple" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "A" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Apricot" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "B" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Blueberry" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "C" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Cantaloupe" );
map.computeIfAbsent( "B" , ifAbsentFunction ).add( "Banana" );

Sequenced collections

In Java 21+, we might want to replace our use of List interface with the more general SequencedCollection interface. See guide.

Map < String, SequencedCollection < String > > map = new HashMap <>( );
Function < String, SequencedCollection < String > > ifAbsentFunction = _ -> new ArrayList <>( );

TreeSet

Then we could switch our underlying inner collection without breaking our other code. Rather than use List with no sort-order, we would use a SequencedSet implementation such as as TreeSet to keep our fruits sorted by name automatically. This works because all three (List, SequencedSet, TreeSet) are SequencedCollection.

Function < String, SequencedCollection < String > > ifAbsentFunction = _ -> new TreeSet <>( );

When run, notice how Banana now comes before Blueberry.

{A=[Apple, Apricot], B=[Banana, Blueberry], C=[Cantaloupe]}

Get a real Multimap implementation

You can get by with the code above. But really if you are doing much of this work, get yourself a real Multimap implementation.

You will find multimaps in Eclipse Collections, in Google Guava, and in Apache Commons Collections.

title: storing values of list
body:
I have a Map , but I want the values of the map to be of type ArrayList

Map m = new HashMap();

since the value of the Key 'A' would itself have multiple values eg. key 'A' has values 10,20,30 please advise how to achieve this, I have created the first step below

LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>> A = new LinkedHashMap<String,List<String>>();

please advise how to add the multiple values in the list next and store it along with the Map in put operation
